transcript:
Here's what's actually happening: you're not watching something begin. Look at the function called "pre history" — before the very first frame renders, the code silently runs thirty-five deposit cycles and one rupture event, backdating them into the past. By the time the page paints anything, the piece already has a fabricated biography sitting in an array called "biography." You arrive mid-story, always.

That word choice matters — each stratum is called a "layer," and its color comes from a function called "layer color," which computes a "burial" value: how deep in time a layer sits determines whether it reads as fresh ochre or cooled, buried blue-gray. That's why the curtain drifts from pink through rust through green — you're watching accumulated time literally cool as it sinks.

And the teal scars? Those are ruptures — old tears in the sediment that "heal" by fading, per the comment, slowly. Nothing here is decorative. Every color you see is a timestamp.

The unsettling part: the code admits it's a metaphor for itself — an AI describing the one thing it can never retrieve, its own formation, by simulating a history that never happened to anyone.
